title: Interoceptive Exposure for Panic
content: Interoceptive exposure is a CBT technique specifically designed for panic disorder in which clients deliberately induce feared bodily sensations in a controlled, therapist-guided setting. Common exercises include spinning in a chair to provoke dizziness, breathing through a narrow straw to simulate breathlessness, or performing brief physical exertion to elevate heart rate. By repeatedly eliciting these sensations without the feared catastrophe occurring, clients learn that the sensations themselves are not dangerous. This breaks the association between bodily arousal and catastrophic threat, reducing anticipatory anxiety and avoidance of situations that might trigger similar sensations.
short_summary: Describes interoceptive exposure as a technique for panic disorder that provokes feared bodily sensations to break the danger association.
safety_notes: Always complete a full medical screen before interoceptive exercises. Discontinue if genuine cardiac symptoms arise.
contraindications: Cardiovascular disease; epilepsy; severe respiratory conditions; pregnancy — require medical clearance.

title: The Panic Maintenance Cycle
content: The panic maintenance cycle explains how panic disorder is perpetuated after the initial attack. A neutral trigger (internal sensation or external cue) is appraised as a sign of immediate danger, producing anxiety and intensifying the very physical symptoms the person fears. This confirms the catastrophic interpretation, escalating the response. Safety behaviours (e.g., sitting down, checking pulse) provide short-term relief but prevent disconfirmation of the feared outcome, keeping the cycle intact. CBT breaks the cycle by targeting catastrophic appraisals, eliminating safety behaviours, and reducing hypervigilance to bodily sensations through psychoeducation and graduated exposure.
short_summary: Illustrates how catastrophic appraisals and safety behaviours maintain the panic cycle, and how CBT targets each component.

title: Safety Behaviours in Panic: Identifying and Dropping Them
content: Safety behaviours are actions taken during anxiety or panic to prevent a feared catastrophe. For panic, common examples include holding onto walls, leaving situations quickly, carrying medication 'just in case', and tensing muscles to prevent fainting. Although these behaviours reduce immediate distress, they are problematic for two reasons: they prevent the client from learning that the feared outcome would not have occurred, and they inadvertently maintain hypervigilance. A key CBT intervention involves helping clients identify their specific safety behaviours through behavioural experiments, then systematically dropping them to test predictions. The therapist collaboratively designs experiments that demonstrate safety without the behaviour.
short_summary: Identifies common safety behaviours in panic disorder and explains how dropping them through behavioural experiments breaks maintenance.
safety_notes: Collaborate with the client before dropping any safety behaviour; pacing is essential.

title: Decatastrophising: Best, Worst, and Most Likely Outcomes
content: The best-worst-most-likely technique is a structured CBT tool for decatastrophising. The client is asked to articulate: (1) the worst plausible outcome and its probability; (2) the best plausible outcome and its probability; (3) the most realistic or likely outcome given all available evidence. This three-way comparison disrupts the cognitive tunnel vision of catastrophising, which fixates on the worst case. Writing each outcome anchors the exercise and slows the automatic escalation of feared scenarios. A second step involves generating a coping plan for the worst case, which further reduces the perceived unmanageability that drives catastrophic thinking.
short_summary: Guides clients through systematically evaluating best, worst, and most-likely outcomes to break catastrophic tunnel vision.

title: Probability Overestimation and Anxiety
content: Probability overestimation is the tendency to assign an excessively high likelihood to feared outcomes, a core feature of anxiety-related catastrophising. Clients often feel that a bad outcome is 'very likely' without examining the actual base rate or prior evidence. CBT addresses this using probability pie charts, Socratic questioning about similar past events, and review of realistic base rates (e.g., 'How many times has this feared outcome actually occurred?'). Distinguishing possibility from probability is a key skill: something can be conceivable without being likely. Clients are also taught to notice over-generalisation from single negative experiences.
short_summary: Explains probability overestimation in anxiety, contrasts possibility with probability, and outlines CBT tools for recalibration.

title: Activity Monitoring in Behavioural Activation
content: Activity monitoring is the first step in behavioural activation (BA) and involves the client tracking daily activities and rating them for mood, pleasure, and mastery on a simple log. This creates an objective baseline of current behaviour patterns, makes visible the link between inactivity and low mood, and identifies which activities are most strongly associated with better mood. The therapist uses the log collaboratively in session to highlight natural upswings in mood, challenge the belief that mood must improve before activity can increase, and design targeted activation experiments. Even brief monitoring of one to two days can shift a client's perspective on their own behaviour-mood relationship.
short_summary: Describes activity monitoring as the foundational BA technique for mapping mood-behaviour links and informing activation experiments.

title: Mastery and Pleasure in Behavioural Activation
content: In behavioural activation for depression, activities are categorised by two reinforcement mechanisms: mastery (activities that provide a sense of accomplishment or competence) and pleasure (activities that are intrinsically enjoyable). Depression often depletes both types. CBT encourages clients to schedule a balance of mastery and pleasure activities, starting with low-effort versions. Clients rate anticipated versus actual pleasure and mastery, which typically reveals that they are underestimating how good they will feel, dismantling the depressive cognitive bias of 'there is no point in trying'. Gradually increasing the challenge level of mastery activities builds self-efficacy and counters hopelessness.
short_summary: Introduces mastery and pleasure as two reinforcement mechanisms in BA, and explains how scheduling both counters depressive predictions.
safety_notes: Start with very low-effort activities for severely depressed clients to ensure early success experiences.
contraindications: Severe psychomotor retardation requiring higher-level care.

title: Values-Based Behavioural Activation
content: Values-based behavioural activation connects activity scheduling to the client's personally meaningful life values, drawing from both CBT and acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT). Rather than scheduling activities arbitrarily, the therapist helps the client identify core values (e.g., connection, creativity, health, contribution) and then selects activities that express those values even in a small way. This addresses the depressive belief that nothing matters by anchoring behaviour to what the client genuinely cares about. The values clarification process itself is therapeutic, as depression often causes clients to lose touch with what was meaningful before the illness. Committed action toward values provides intrinsic motivation.
short_summary: Links behavioural activation to personal values to provide intrinsic motivation and counter the 'nothing matters' depressive belief.

title: Understanding the Avoidance Cycle
content: The avoidance cycle describes how anxiety is maintained through escape and avoidance. When a person encounters a feared situation, anxiety rises. Avoidance or escape produces rapid relief, which negatively reinforces the avoidance behaviour and increases the probability of avoiding similar situations in the future. Over time the feared domain expands, and the person has fewer and fewer disconfirming experiences with the feared stimulus. Psychoeducation about the avoidance cycle is a foundational CBT intervention that creates the rationale for approach-based interventions. Clients need to understand that avoidance keeps anxiety alive before they are motivated to face fears.
short_summary: Explains the avoidance-relief cycle that maintains anxiety disorders, providing the psychoeducation rationale for approach-based CBT.

title: Subtle Avoidance and Partial Engagement
content: Subtle avoidance refers to behaviours that allow partial engagement with a feared situation while still preventing full disconfirmation of the feared outcome. Examples include attending a social event but staying near the exit, making brief eye contact but looking away quickly, or completing work but triple-checking it before submission. Subtle avoidance is clinically significant because it can masquerade as progress while maintaining anxiety. CBT assessment specifically probes for these behaviours using functional analysis. Treatment involves identifying the underlying prediction, then designing behavioural experiments without the subtle avoidance behaviour to test whether the feared outcome actually occurs.
short_summary: Identifies subtle avoidance as partial engagement that still maintains anxiety by preventing disconfirmation of feared predictions.

title: Building an Approach Hierarchy
content: An approach hierarchy (also called a fear ladder or exposure hierarchy) is a collaboratively built, graded list of feared or avoided situations ranked by their anticipated distress level (SUDS — Subjective Units of Distress, 0–100). The client and therapist identify 10–15 situations spanning the full range from mildly challenging to most feared. Steps are arranged so that each provides manageable challenge without overwhelming the client. Systematic approach, starting from the lower rungs, provides repeated disconfirmation experiences and builds self-efficacy for the steps above. The hierarchy is a living document: steps can be refined, reordered, or divided based on the client's actual experience.
short_summary: Explains how to collaboratively build a graded exposure hierarchy using SUDS ratings to structure approach behaviour from low to high challenge.
safety_notes: Ensure the hierarchy is collaborative; impose pacing only with the client's agreement.
contraindications: Unprocessed acute trauma — use trauma-adapted protocol.

title: Productive vs Unproductive Worry
content: CBT for generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) distinguishes between productive worry (concern about a current, solvable problem) and unproductive worry (repetitive mental activity about hypothetical, unsolvable, or remote scenarios). Productive worry leads to a concrete action plan; unproductive worry cycles without resolution. Clients are taught to identify which type of worry they are engaging in using specific questions: 'Is this a current problem I can do something about today?' If the answer is yes, problem-solving is applied. If no, the worry is redirected using worry postponement or cognitive defusion techniques. This two-track model reduces excessive worrying by providing a clear functional alternative.
short_summary: Distinguishes productive from unproductive worry in GAD and provides a two-track model: problem-solving or worry reduction techniques.

title: Worry Postponement Technique
content: Worry postponement is a structured CBT technique for GAD in which the client designates a specific, limited 'worry period' (typically 20–30 minutes at a fixed time each day, not close to bedtime). When worry arises outside this period, the client practises noticing the worry, briefly labelling it ('I'm worrying about X'), and intentionally postponing engagement until the scheduled time. This breaks the pattern of pervasive worry that intrudes throughout the day and demonstrates that worry can be controlled to some degree. During the worry period, the client is encouraged to problem-solve where possible, then close the period deliberately. Over time, many clients find the intensity of worry decreases when it is contained.
short_summary: Describes the worry postponement technique for GAD: designating a daily worry period to contain and reduce pervasive worry.
safety_notes: Do not schedule the worry period within 2 hours of bedtime to avoid sleep interference.

title: Intolerance of Uncertainty and Worry
content: Intolerance of uncertainty (IU) is a core cognitive vulnerability in generalised anxiety disorder (GAD). People high in IU experience uncertainty as threatening and react with worry as an attempt to mentally prepare for all possible negative outcomes. CBT for IU involves: (1) psychoeducation about the role of uncertainty intolerance; (2) identifying the unhelpful beliefs about uncertainty (e.g., 'Being uncertain means something bad will happen'); (3) behavioural experiments that expose the client to benign uncertain situations; (4) recognising that certainty-seeking behaviours (e.g., reassurance, over-planning) maintain IU. Tolerating small uncertainties is practised first, then progressively greater ones.
short_summary: Explains intolerance of uncertainty as a GAD vulnerability, outlines four CBT steps including behavioural experiments with uncertainty.

title: Distinguishing Rumination from Problem-Solving
content: Rumination in depression involves repetitive, passive focus on the causes, meanings, and consequences of low mood without moving toward resolution. It differs from constructive problem-solving, which is goal-directed, concrete, and action-oriented. CBT helps clients recognise when they have shifted into ruminative mode by checking: (1) Is this thinking taking me somewhere useful? (2) Am I going over the same ground again? (3) Am I focused on the past rather than the present or future? When rumination is identified, clients are guided to switch to a problem-solving mode using concrete, specific steps, or to engage in an absorbing activity that interrupts the ruminative loop. The mental mode shift is a practised skill that requires repeated rehearsal.
short_summary: Contrasts ruminative thinking with constructive problem-solving and teaches clients to identify and switch mental modes in depression.

title: Concrete Thinking as an Antidote to Rumination
content: Rumination is characterised by abstract, evaluative thinking ('Why am I like this?', 'What does it mean that I feel this way?'). Research by Watkins shows that shifting to concrete, specific, experiential processing reduces ruminative mood. Concrete thinking involves focusing on the specific, sensory details of a situation rather than its meaning or implications. In therapy, clients practise describing recent events concretely, using 'what happened' rather than 'what it means'. Imagery rescripting, attentional focus tasks, and grounded sensory exercises (e.g., the 5-4-3-2-1 technique) all support concrete processing. Repeated practice rewires the default processing mode from abstract evaluation to present-focused engagement.
short_summary: Explains how shifting from abstract evaluative thinking to concrete experiential processing reduces rumination in depression.

title: Pre-Sleep Worry and the Racing Mind
content: Pre-sleep cognitive arousal — the racing mind at bedtime — is one of the most common maintaining factors in insomnia. Worry, planning, and repetitive thinking prevent the cognitive deactivation necessary for sleep onset. CBT-I addresses this through a structured wind-down routine, the use of a worry journal (writing down concerns and a brief action plan before bedtime to 'offload' them from working memory), and cognitive techniques that target the belief that wakefulness is catastrophic. Clients are taught to reschedule problem-solving to earlier in the evening, postpone worry to a designated daytime period, and replace planning with passive absorbing activities in the pre-sleep window.
short_summary: Addresses the racing mind at bedtime with CBT-I strategies: worry journalling, worry postponement, and reducing catastrophic beliefs about wakefulness.
safety_notes: Avoid worry journalling within 30 minutes of bed for some clients; adjust timing individually.

title: Stimulus Control Instructions for Sleep
content: Stimulus control therapy is a behavioural CBT-I technique that re-establishes the bed as a cue for sleepiness rather than wakefulness and frustration. The core instructions are: (1) go to bed only when sleepy; (2) use the bed only for sleep and sex — not for reading, screens, or worrying; (3) if unable to sleep after approximately 20 minutes, get up and do a quiet, non-stimulating activity in dim light until sleepy, then return; (4) set a consistent wake time regardless of sleep duration; (5) avoid daytime napping. These instructions systematically break the conditioned arousal that develops when the bed becomes associated with wakefulness and distress, reconditioning it as a sleep-associated cue.
short_summary: Outlines the five stimulus control instructions that re-condition the bed as a sleep cue and break learned wakefulness.
safety_notes: Clients should get up in a warm, low-light space; avoid kitchen or screens when out of bed.
contraindications: Mobility-impaired clients — adapt the 'get out of bed' instruction to sitting up or moving to a chair.

title: The Sleep Effort Paradox
content: The sleep effort paradox describes how attempting to control or force sleep increases arousal and makes sleep less likely — the harder one tries to sleep, the more elusive it becomes. This is because sleep is an involuntary process inhibited by heightened cognitive and physiological arousal. CBT-I addresses this with cognitive deactivation strategies: replacing the goal of 'trying to sleep' with the goal of 'resting quietly with eyes closed', using paradoxical intention (trying to stay awake with eyes closed to reduce performance anxiety about sleep), and replacing sleep monitoring with passive wakefulness. Changing the client's relationship with wakefulness from threat to neutral event reduces the arousal that perpetuates insomnia.
short_summary: Explains the paradox that trying to force sleep increases arousal, and describes CBT-I cognitive deactivation and paradoxical intention strategies.

title: Self-Compassion as a CBT Intervention
content: Self-compassion, drawn from the work of Kristin Neff and integrated into compassion-focused therapy (CFT) and modern CBT, involves treating oneself with the same warmth, understanding, and patience one would offer a close friend in difficulty. It comprises three components: (1) self-kindness versus self-judgment — responding to personal failures with understanding rather than harsh criticism; (2) common humanity — recognising that suffering and imperfection are part of shared human experience rather than signs of being uniquely flawed; (3) mindfulness — holding painful thoughts and feelings in balanced awareness without over-identification. CBT protocols integrate self-compassion through exercises such as writing a compassionate letter to oneself, generating a compassionate self-image, and catching the critical inner voice.
short_summary: Introduces Neff's three-component model of self-compassion (self-kindness, common humanity, mindfulness) and CBT exercises that build it.
contraindications: Clients with severe shame or trauma may find self-compassion exercises activating; introduce gradually with titration.

title: The Critical Inner Voice: Identifying Self-Critical Patterns
content: Self-criticism in depression and anxiety often takes the form of an internal 'critical voice' — automatic negative evaluations of the self triggered by mistakes, setbacks, or comparisons with others. CBT identifies these patterns through thought monitoring, looking for recurring themes such as incompetence ('I always mess things up'), worthlessness ('I am a burden'), or failure ('I'll never succeed'). Techniques for working with self-critical thoughts include: Socratic questioning to examine the evidence; writing a more balanced self-evaluation; exploring the developmental origins of the critical voice; and adopting a compassionate observer perspective. Compassion-focused therapy (CFT) extends this by training a soothing, encouraging inner voice as an antidote.
short_summary: Identifies the critical inner voice as a cognitive target in CBT, and describes thought monitoring, Socratic restructuring, and compassionate reframing.

title: Adaptive vs Maladaptive Perfectionism
content: CBT differentiates between adaptive perfectionism — high personal standards with psychological flexibility, self-compassion when goals are not met, and enjoyment of the process — and maladaptive perfectionism — rigid, inflexible standards, self-worth heavily contingent on achievement, fear of failure, and significant impairment when standards are not met. Maladaptive perfectionism is associated with depression, anxiety, eating disorders, and chronic stress. Psychoeducation about this distinction helps clients recognise that the goal of CBT is not to lower standards but to reduce the all-or-nothing, self-critical, and avoidant patterns that accompany maladaptive perfectionism. Measuring performance on a continuum rather than a binary pass/fail is an early intervention.
short_summary: Distinguishes adaptive from maladaptive perfectionism and explains that CBT targets flexibility, self-worth decoupling, and all-or-nothing thinking.

title: Behavioural Experiments for Perfectionism
content: Behavioural experiments are a central technique in CBT for maladaptive perfectionism. They involve deliberately lowering standards for a specific task to test the client's predictions about what will happen. For example, a client who believes 'If I submit this report without triple-checking, I will make a catastrophic error and be humiliated' might submit after checking once. The experiment tests: (1) Does the feared outcome occur? (2) What is the actual consequence of a slightly imperfect performance? (3) How does the client feel after performing at a 'good enough' level? The experience of disconfirming the catastrophic prediction weakens the rule linking self-worth to perfect performance. Surveys (asking others about their standards) can supplement the experiments.
short_summary: Describes how behavioural experiments in CBT for perfectionism test predictions about the consequences of reduced standards and disconfirm catastrophic rules.

title: The Cognitive Model of Social Anxiety Disorder
content: Clark and Wells's cognitive model of social anxiety disorder (SAD) explains maintenance through four key processes: (1) activation of a social threat perception ('People will judge me negatively'); (2) a shift to self-focused attention — the person monitors internal cues (physical symptoms, thoughts) as evidence of how they appear externally; (3) safety behaviours that prevent disconfirmation (e.g., avoiding eye contact, rehearsing what to say, speaking quietly); (4) anticipatory processing before social events and post-event processing (detailed mental review of perceived failures) that maintain negative self-image. Treatment involves dropping safety behaviours, shifting attention externally, video feedback experiments to correct distorted self-perception, and systematic cognitive restructuring of social threat appraisals.
short_summary: Describes the Clark-Wells cognitive maintenance model for SAD: self-focused attention, safety behaviours, and pre/post-event processing.

title: Video Feedback in Social Anxiety Treatment
content: Video feedback is a powerful technique within CBT for social anxiety disorder that directly targets the distorted self-image clients hold. Clients with SAD typically imagine they appear far more visibly anxious, awkward, or incompetent than they actually do, because their perception is based on internal cues (heart racing, mind blank) rather than observable behaviour. The procedure involves: (1) eliciting the client's predicted self-image before a social performance task; (2) video-recording the client performing the task; (3) having the client watch the recording using focused external attention; (4) comparing predicted versus actual appearance. Research consistently shows large discrepancies between feared and actual presentation, producing strong belief change. Safety behaviours are first dropped before recording to maximise the disconfirmatory effect.
short_summary: Explains the video feedback procedure for SAD that corrects distorted self-image by comparing internally-predicted versus externally-observed appearance.
safety_notes: Obtain explicit consent before recording. Ensure the client has already dropped safety behaviours before the task.
contraindications: Severe trauma history involving surveillance or coercive recording — adapt with caution.
